food_subgroup: Noodle dishes — dry-tossed ramen with fried yuba (tofu skin)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002802 covers ramen noodle dish as a category. No exact code exists for dry-tossed ramen with fried yuba; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code for dry ramen with yuba exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific fusion noodle preparation.

main_ingredients: fried yuba (tofu skin / bean curd skin)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Fried Yuba Dry Ramen is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of cooked ramen noodles tossed in a speciality soy-sesame sauce and topped with crispy fried yuba (tofu skin).

nutrient_source.title: Ramen noodles, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; Tofu skin (yuba), fried — FAO/INFOODS Asia nutrient composition data; composite estimation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Fried Yuba Dry Ramen as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from: (1) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked ramen/wheat noodles (FDC ID 168917), providing the noodle base macros; (2) FAO/INFOODS SEA data for fried tofu skin (yuba) providing protein and fat uplift; (3) soy-sesame sauce contribution estimated from USDA SR Legacy soy sauce and sesame oil entries. Weighted composite per 100 g of finished dish (approximately 55% noodles, 20% fried yuba, 25% sauce and garnish). Values are moderate-confidence estimates.

gi_source.title: Noodles, instant, dried, boiled — Glycemic Index Database; wheat noodle GI reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Fried Yuba Dry Ramen. GI of 52 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat-based Asian noodles / instant ramen noodles (boiled), which report GI values in the range 47–58 (mean ~52). The fried yuba topping and soy-sesame sauce do not materially alter the GI of the noodle base. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 89.25) / 100 ≈ 46.4, rounded to 46.7 using 25.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 350 g serving = 89.25 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1820 mg per serving) from soy-based tossing sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a single serving may contribute approximately 79–91% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~52) and high glycemic load per serving (~46.7) due to large noodle portion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite a low-to-moderate GI, the high carbohydrate load per restaurant serving (~89 g) produces a high glycemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion or substitute with a lower-carbohydrate noodle option and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content; fried yuba contributes unsaturated fats but also caloric density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains moderate total fat (7.2 g/100 g) with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated ratio, but the high sodium content from the soy sauce base is a primary cardiovascular concern; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and request lower-sodium sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Yuba Dry Ramen is a Japanese-inspired fusion noodle dish served in Singapore, consisting of wheat-based ramen noodles tossed in a speciality soy-sesame sauce without broth, topped with crispy deep-fried yuba (tofu skin / bean curd skin). Yuba is the thin film that forms on the surface of heated soy milk during tofu production; when deep-fried it becomes golden, crispy, and rich in plant protein. The dry-tossed style (similar to Japanese mazemen or abura soba) concentrates the umami flavour of the sauce directly onto the noodles. The dish has a chewy noodle texture contrasted by the crunchy yuba topping, with a savoury, slightly nutty, and mildly spicy flavour profile depending on the sauce formulation. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein (~7.8 g/100 g) largely from both the wheat noodles and the soy-derived yuba, moderate fat (~7.2 g/100 g) with a favourable unsaturated fatty acid profile from sesame oil and yuba, and a significant carbohydrate load (~25.5 g/100 g) from the noodle base. Sodium is elevated (~520 mg/100 g) due to the soy-based sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low-moderate), but the glycemic load per restaurant serving is high (~46.7) due to the large noodle portion, which is clinically relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is vegetarian-friendly (no meat or egg in the base preparation) and dairy-free, but contains gluten and soy allergens. It is served at select Singapore restaurants including Flavours by Saute at Funan mall.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry covers Fried Yuba Dry Ramen as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (wheat noodles) and FAO/INFOODS SEA (fried tofu skin) with weighted composite methodology. GI is analogously estimated from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle entries. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. Estimation basis: ~55% cooked wheat ramen noodles (USDA FDC SR Legacy), ~20% fried yuba/tofu skin (FAO/INFOODS SEA), ~25% soy-sesame sauce and garnish (USDA SR Legacy). 2. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources for this dish type. 3. Vitamin B6 (vitamin_b6_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA fried tofu skin entry and composite estimation is insufficiently reliable. 4. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is -1 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients in the base; B12 is not present in meaningful quantities and not reported in source data. 5. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 6. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this dish type. 7. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources. 8. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy entries used. 9. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg as this dish contains no animal-derived ingredients in the standard base preparation; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for wheat noodles and FAO/INFOODS for fried tofu skin. 10. Trans fat is 0.0 g; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for the noodle and oil components. 11. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle entries (range 47–58); no direct GI measurement for this dish exists. 12. Halal status is unconfirmed for the restaurant preparation; marked false pending certification verification. 13. Vegetarian flag is true for the base dish; individual restaurant preparations may vary.
